title: Share of cancer deaths attributed to risk factors
subtitle: The estimated share of deaths from [cancers](#dod:cancer) attributed to all [risk factors](#dod:risk-factor). The remaining share represents deaths that would be expected to have occurred in the absence of these known risk factors.
caption:
Share of cancer deaths attributed to risk factors, World, 2023

The estimated share of deaths from cancers attributed to all risk factors. The remaining share represents deaths that would be expected to have occurred in the absence of these known risk factors.

This horizontal bar chart ranks 22 cancer types by the percentage of global deaths in 2023 attributed to known risk factors, with values ranging from 100% down to 0.9%. Cervical cancer sits at the top of the distribution with a full 100% of deaths linked to risk factors, followed closely by mesothelioma at 91.7% and lung, tracheal and bronchus cancers at 76.4%. Larynx cancer registers 73.4%, while esophageal and colon and rectum cancers both fall in the mid-50% range at 56.4% and 56.2% respectively. Liver cancer shows 48.2% of deaths tied to risk factors, placing it near the middle of the scale. Lower down, lip and oral cavity cancers stand at 39.4%, pancreatic at 38.7%, uterine at 33.3%, and bladder at 33%. Breast cancer records 29.8%, kidney 27.9%, nasopharynx 23.7%, stomach 19%, leukemia 18.7%, thyroid 11.6%, gallbladder and biliary tract 11.4%, multiple myeloma 7.6%, non-Hodgkin lymphoma 4.8%, and prostate cancer at the bottom with just 0.9%. The metric is age-standardized to allow comparisons between countries and over time. Not all deaths in a country may have been registered with a cause of death. In some cases, IHME methodology for calculating risks can include negative values, which can reflect potential protective effects or indicate uncertainty due to weak or poorly understood relationships between the risk factors and health outcomes. The data shows a steep gradient from cervical cancer at the maximum to prostate cancer at the minimum, with most cancer types clustered below 50%.
